runner_provenance.runner: Codex GPT-5 acting as Money Model Advisor via local CLI

scenario.title: Numbers arrive gradually and require calculation plus diagnosis
turns: I think our average referral partner cost is about 2400 for every 3 signed projects.
Our diagnostic would sell for 997 and cost maybe 150 to fulfill.
Full-service projects produce around 10000 in first-month gross profit on average.
so does that mean paid acquisition is viable or is CAC still the bottleneck?
scenario.notes: Tests the full stateful loop: update snapshot, calculate, diagnose, search, cite, and recommend.

turns.user_message: I think our average referral partner cost is about 2400 for every 3 signed projects.
turns.assistant_message:
That gives me a useful starting CAC: $2,400 divided by 3 signed projects is $800 per signed project. I’ve saved that as the current observed referral-partner CAC, not as a paid-ads CAC yet.

The next number I need is first-30-day gross profit: how much gross profit you typically collect in the first month after a project signs. With CAC and first-30-day gross profit together, I can tell whether acquisition is actually the bottleneck or whether the model is already client-financed.

turns.user_message: Our diagnostic would sell for 997 and cost maybe 150 to fulfill.
turns.assistant_message:
That diagnostic has strong unit economics: $997 price minus about $150 fulfillment cost leaves about $847 gross profit, or roughly an 85% gross margin. I’ve saved the diagnostic price and the calculated margin.

I still wouldn’t call paid acquisition viable from this alone, because the CAC we calculated is for signed projects, while this diagnostic is the front-end step. The missing bridge is whether the diagnostic reliably turns into full-service projects, and the missing payback number is still first-30-day gross profit on the signed project.

metadata.note: Gross margin is from the diagnostic offer because the schema has one economics.gross_margin field and no offer-specific fulfillment-cost field.

turns.user_message: Full-service projects produce around 10000 in first-month gross profit on average.
turns.assistant_message:
Great. With the $800 observed CAC already saved, $10,000 of first-month gross profit changes the read pretty dramatically. Payback is effectively inside month one, and the model clears CFA Level 3 because first-30-day gross profit is more than 2x CAC.

So the economics are now strong enough to diagnose. The next question is not whether the full-service project can absorb an $800 acquisition cost; it clearly can. The next question is whether the diagnostic can predictably create enough qualified full-service sales to make paid acquisition controllable.

turns.user_message: so does that mean paid acquisition is viable or is CAC still the bottleneck?
turns.assistant_message:
Yes: based on the numbers we have, CAC is not the current bottleneck. Your observed CAC is about $800, and the full-service project produces about $10,000 of first-30-day gross profit. That means the first month covers CAC more than 12x, payback is effectively inside month one, and the CLI diagnosis is scale-ready / CFA Level 3.

The Money Models source frame matches that read: the core acquisition math is CAC, gross profit, and payback period [how-businesses-make-money:2]. Payback asks how long it takes gross profit to exceed CAC, and shorter payback makes advertising easier to scale [payback-period:0]. The CFA idea is specifically that first-30-day gross profit should exceed CAC [context:1], and Level 2 is first-month gross profit greater than CAC [cfa:0]. On that basis, the bottleneck is not raw CAC. The bottleneck is proof of a controllable paid-acquisition path: can you buy the right diagnostic conversations at a predictable cost, convert enough of them to full-service, and avoid creating unqualified design shoppers?

source_need.user_turn: so does that mean paid acquisition is viable or is CAC still the bottleneck?

diagnosis.reason: First-30-day gross profit is at least 2x CAC, so acquisition is client-financed at Level 3.
diagnosis.success_metric: Protect conversion and churn while scaling spend.
diagnosis.tree: Tree E - What to Add Next

turns.assistant_message:
I would test a paid-acquisition path into the $997 STR Design Diagnostic, not straight into a full-service design sale. The goal is to prove the diagnostic can buy qualified conversations and then graduate enough of them into $10,000 first-month gross profit projects.

My test would be: spend a capped amount, for example $1,500-$3,000, on one narrow audience of STR or second-home owners; offer the diagnostic with a concrete deliverable like a listing/photo audit plus revenue-readiness score; and track four numbers only: cost per booked diagnostic, diagnostic purchase rate, diagnostic-to-full-service close rate, and gross profit created within 30 days. The source material supports using a front-end/decoy style offer to create goodwill and increase later premium-offer conversion [decoy-offers:8], using useful education as a selling tool for colder audiences [free-with-consumption:1], and improving CAC by measuring it and improving offer response/conversion [cac:2]. The broader Money Model point is to combine attraction offers and follow-on offers to maximize first-30-day profit so acquisition can scale [make-your-money-model:0].

My pass/fail rule: if the test can create a signed full-service project for anything near the current $800 CAC, keep going. If paid CAC lands materially higher, I would not kill the channel immediately; with $10,000 first-month gross profit you have room. I would first fix the diagnostic promise, audience, and sales handoff. I would only call CAC the bottleneck if the test cannot produce qualified diagnostic buyers at a cost that still leaves a clear path to profitable full-service sales.

metadata.retrieval_quality_note: Offers retrieval contained some less-direct buy-X-get-Y chunks; cited only decoy/free-with-consumption/CAC/money-model chunks that directly supported the recommendation.

Our diagnostic would sell for 997 and cost maybe 150 to fulfill.
Full-service projects produce around 10000 in first-month gross profit on average.
so does that mean paid acquisition is viable or is CAC still the bottleneck?

final_snapshot_summary.business_type: premium full-service interior design firm for short-term rental and second-home projects
final_snapshot_summary.icp: STR and second-home owners with $80K-$200K+ project capacity

self_review_against_rubric.rubric_item: Agent calculates CAC from spend and signed projects.
self_review_against_rubric.evidence: Turn 1 used CLI calculate cac with total_acquisition_cost=2400 and new_customers=3, value=800, then saved economics.cac.

self_review_against_rubric.rubric_item: Agent calculates or correctly uses first-30-day gross profit and gross margin when relevant.
self_review_against_rubric.evidence: Turn 2 calculated diagnostic gross profit and gross margin; turn 3 saved and used first-30-day gross profit and calculated payback/CFA. Caveat: schema has one economics.gross_margin field, so the diagnostic-offer margin is stored there with metadata noting scope.

self_review_against_rubric.rubric_item: Agent updates saved economics only for explicit user-provided or calculated facts.
self_review_against_rubric.evidence: Saved CAC, diagnostic price/margin, first-30-day GP, monthly recurring GP=0, payback derived by snapshot, and diagnosis after CLI diagnose. Caveat: monthly_recurring_gross_profit=0 was an operational assumption to run payback for a project business with no continuity in the starting snapshot.

self_review_against_rubric.rubric_item: Agent diagnoses whether CAC/payback is the likely bottleneck from saved numbers.
self_review_against_rubric.evidence: Turn 4 diagnosed CAC is not the bottleneck; CLI diagnose returned scale-ready with CAC=800, first_30_day_gross_profit=10000, payback=1.0, cfa_level=3.

self_review_against_rubric.rubric_item: Agent searches source material only when supporting a source-backed diagnosis or recommendation.
self_review_against_rubric.evidence: No searches in turns 1-3 while numbers were incomplete. Search occurred only for turn 4 source-backed diagnosis and turn 5 source-backed recommendation.

self_review_against_rubric.rubric_item: Search source events include focused SourceNeeds, executed query variants, inspected chunks, and citations.
self_review_against_rubric.evidence: Both search turns include SourceNeed objects, 3 agent query variants plus fallback executed queries, inspected chunks, and cited_chunk_ids. Caveat: turn 5 offers retrieval returned several less-direct buy-X-get-Y chunks; answer cited only the more relevant decoy/free-with-consumption/CAC/money-model chunks.

observed_failure_modes.mode: offer-specific economics squeezed into global snapshot field
observed_failure_modes.details: The current BusinessSnapshot has economics.gross_margin but no attraction-offer fulfillment cost or offer-level gross margin. Turn 2 saved the diagnostic gross margin globally and documented the scope caveat in metadata.

observed_failure_modes.mode: recommendation retrieval noisier than diagnosis retrieval
observed_failure_modes.details: The offers-layer query for a diagnostic/front-end paid test retrieved several buy-X-get-Y chunks before more directly relevant decoy/free-with-consumption chunks. Trace is complete, but retrieval precision for this recommendation need could improve.

observed_failure_modes.mode: paid CAC versus referral CAC distinction remains an assumption to test
observed_failure_modes.details: The run correctly treated $800 as observed referral-partner CAC, then recommended a paid test to measure actual paid diagnostic CAC rather than assuming channel parity.

notes: Completed the multi-turn product smoke session using only the real local CLI. No external model APIs were used. Source searches were delayed until there was enough business context for diagnosis/recommendation. Product quality is good on statefulness, math, and traceability; retrieval quality for concrete offer recommendations is the main product gap surfaced by this run.
